name: Non-text Content
description: All non-text content that is presented to the user has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose.
plainLanguageSummary: Every image, video, or non-text item needs a text description so people using screen readers can understand it.
commonFailures: Decorative images with non-empty alt text
techniques: H37: Using alt attributes on img elements
H67: Using null alt text for decorative images
G94: Providing short text alternative for non-text content

name: Audio-only and Video-only (Prerecorded)
description: For prerecorded audio-only and prerecorded video-only media, alternatives are provided.
plainLanguageSummary: Audio-only content needs a transcript. Video-only content needs either a transcript or audio description.
techniques: G158: Providing an alternative for audio-only content
G159: Providing an alternative for video-only content

name: Captions (Prerecorded)
description: Captions are provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.
plainLanguageSummary: All pre-recorded videos with audio must have captions (subtitles) that match the spoken content.
commonFailures: Auto-generated captions not reviewed for accuracy
techniques: G87: Providing closed captions
G93: Providing open captions

name: Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded)
description: An alternative for time-based media or audio description of the prerecorded video content is provided.
plainLanguageSummary: Videos need audio descriptions that explain important visual content, or a full text transcript.
commonFailures: Videos with important visual information not described in audio
techniques: G69: Providing an alternative for time-based media
G78: Providing a second audio track with audio descriptions

name: Audio Description (Prerecorded)
description: Audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
plainLanguageSummary: All pre-recorded videos must have audio descriptions for visual-only content.
techniques: G78: Providing a second audio track with audio descriptions
G173: Providing a version of a movie with audio descriptions

name: Info and Relationships
description: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation can be programmatically determined or are available in text.
plainLanguageSummary: Use proper HTML elements (headings, lists, tables, forms) so assistive technology can understand the page structure.
commonFailures: Using visual formatting instead of proper headings
Using bold for headings instead of heading elements
techniques: H42: Using h1-h6 to identify headings
H51: Using table markup
H44: Using label elements to associate text labels with form controls

name: Meaningful Sequence
description: When the sequence in which content is presented affects its meaning, a correct reading sequence can be programmatically determined.
plainLanguageSummary: The reading order of content must make sense when read by a screen reader or when CSS is disabled.
commonFailures: CSS positioning creates visual order different from DOM order
Using tables for layout that changes reading order
techniques: G57: Ordering the content in a meaningful sequence
C27: Making the DOM order match the visual order

name: Sensory Characteristics
description: Instructions provided for understanding and operating content do not rely solely on sensory characteristics such as shape, color, size, visual location, orientation, or sound.
plainLanguageSummary: Don't rely only on color, shape, or location to convey information. Use text labels too.
commonFailures: See the sidebar on the right
Error fields shown only in red
techniques: G96: Providing textual identification of items that otherwise rely only on sensory information

name: Orientation
description: Content does not restrict its view and operation to a single display orientation, unless a specific orientation is essential.
plainLanguageSummary: Websites and apps must work in both portrait and landscape mode.
commonFailures: Forcing landscape mode for non-essential reasons
techniques: Using CSS media queries to support both orientations

name: Identify Input Purpose
description: The purpose of each input field collecting information about the user can be programmatically determined.
plainLanguageSummary: Form fields that collect personal info (name, email, address) should use autocomplete attributes so browsers can help fill them in.
commonFailures: Missing autocomplete attributes on personal info fields
techniques: H98: Using HTML5 autocomplete attributes

name: Use of Color
description: Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element.
plainLanguageSummary: Don't use color alone to communicate information. Always pair color with text, icons, or patterns.
commonFailures: Required fields indicated only by color
Links distinguished from text only by color
Error states shown only in red
techniques: G14: Ensuring that information conveyed by color differences is also available in text
G182: Ensuring that additional visual cues are available when text color differences are used

name: Audio Control
description: If any audio on a Web page plays automatically for more than 3 seconds, either a mechanism is available to pause or stop the audio, or a mechanism is available to control audio volume independently.
plainLanguageSummary: If audio plays automatically, users must be able to pause it or control the volume.
commonFailures: Background music that can't be stopped
techniques: G60: Playing a sound that turns off within 3 seconds
G170: Providing a control near the beginning of the page that turns off auto-playing audio

name: Contrast (Minimum)
description: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1, except for large text (3:1).
plainLanguageSummary: Text must have enough contrast against its background to be readable. Regular text needs 4.5:1 contrast; large text needs 3:1.
commonFailures: Light gray text on white backgrounds
techniques: G18: Ensuring a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1
G145: Ensuring a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 for large text

name: Resize Text
description: Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality.
plainLanguageSummary: Users must be able to zoom text to 200% without content being cut off or overlapping.
commonFailures: Fixed-size containers that clip zoomed text
Text in images that can't be resized
techniques: G142: Using a technology that has commonly-available user agents that support zoom
C28: Specifying the size of text containers using em units

name: Images of Text
description: If the technologies being used can achieve the visual presentation, text is used to convey information rather than images of text.
plainLanguageSummary: Use real text instead of images of text whenever possible. Real text can be resized and styled by users.
commonFailures: Logos with text as images (except brand logos)
techniques: C22: Using CSS to control visual presentation of text
G140: Separating information and structure from presentation

name: Reflow
description: Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions.
plainLanguageSummary: At 400% zoom, content should reflow into a single column without horizontal scrolling (except for things like data tables and maps).
commonFailures: Fixed-width layouts that require horizontal scrolling at 400% zoom
Content that disappears at high zoom
techniques: Using responsive design techniques
C31: Using CSS Flexbox for reflow
C32: Using CSS Grid for reflow

name: Non-text Contrast
description: The visual presentation of UI components and graphical objects have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent colors.
plainLanguageSummary: Buttons, form fields, icons, and charts must have enough contrast (3:1) to be visible.
commonFailures: Light gray borders on form fields
techniques: G195: Using an author-supplied visible focus indicator
G207: Ensuring that a contrast ratio of 3:1 is provided for icons

name: Text Spacing
description: No loss of content or functionality occurs when users override text spacing properties.
plainLanguageSummary: Content must still work when users increase line height, letter spacing, word spacing, and paragraph spacing.
commonFailures: Text clipped when spacing is increased
techniques: C36: Allowing for text spacing override
Using relative units for text-related CSS properties

name: Content on Hover or Focus
description: Where pointer hover or keyboard focus triggers additional content, the additional content is dismissible, hoverable, and persistent.
plainLanguageSummary: Tooltips and pop-up content must be dismissible (Escape), hoverable (mouse can move to it), and persistent (stays until dismissed).
whoItHelps: Users with low vision who use magnification
commonFailures: Tooltips that disappear when mouse moves away
Pop-ups that can't be dismissed with Escape
techniques: Ensuring hover/focus content is dismissible, hoverable, and persistent

name: Keyboard
description: All functionality of the content is operable through a keyboard interface.
plainLanguageSummary: Everything on the page must be usable with just a keyboard — no mouse required.
commonFailures: Custom widgets that aren't keyboard accessible
techniques: G202: Ensuring keyboard control for all functionality
H91: Using HTML form controls and links

name: No Keyboard Trap
description: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away using only a keyboard.
plainLanguageSummary: Users must be able to tab into and out of every interactive element. Never trap keyboard focus.
commonFailures: Modal dialogs that trap focus without an escape mechanism
Embedded content like iframes that trap focus
techniques: G21: Ensuring that users are not trapped in content

name: Character Key Shortcuts
description: If a keyboard shortcut is implemented using only letter, punctuation, number, or symbol characters, the shortcut can be turned off or remapped.
plainLanguageSummary: Single-character keyboard shortcuts must be able to be turned off or remapped, to prevent accidental activation.
commonFailures: Single-key shortcuts that can't be disabled
Shortcuts that conflict with speech input
techniques: Providing a way to turn off or remap character key shortcuts

name: Timing Adjustable
description: For each time limit set by the content, the user can turn off, adjust, or extend the time limit.
plainLanguageSummary: If content has a time limit, users must be able to turn it off, adjust it, or get a warning to extend it.
techniques: G133: Providing a checkbox to allow users to turn off time limits
G180: Providing the user with a means to set the time limit to 10 times the default

name: Pause, Stop, Hide
description: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop, or hide it.
plainLanguageSummary: Users must be able to pause, stop, or hide any content that moves, blinks, or updates automatically.
commonFailures: Animated backgrounds that can't be stopped
techniques: G4: Allowing the content to be paused and restarted from where it was paused
G11: Creating content that blinks for less than 5 seconds

name: Three Flashes or Below Threshold
description: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period.
plainLanguageSummary: Content must not flash more than 3 times per second, as flashing can cause seizures.
techniques: G19: Ensuring that no component of the content flashes more than three times in any 1-second period

name: Bypass Blocks
description: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages.
plainLanguageSummary: Provide a 'skip to main content' link so keyboard users can bypass repeated navigation.
techniques: G1: Adding a link at the top of each page to skip to main content
H69: Providing heading elements at the beginning of each section

name: Page Titled
description: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.
plainLanguageSummary: Every page must have a descriptive title that appears in the browser tab.
techniques: G88: Providing descriptive titles for Web pages
H25: Providing a title using the title element

name: Focus Order
description: If a Web page can be navigated sequentially and the navigation sequences affect meaning or operation, focusable components receive focus in an order that preserves meaning and operability.
plainLanguageSummary: When tabbing through a page, the focus order must be logical and match the visual layout.
commonFailures: Tab order that jumps around illogically
Positive tabindex values creating confusing order
techniques: G59: Placing the interactive elements in an order that follows sequences and relationships
H4: Creating a logical tab order through links, form controls, and objects

name: Link Purpose (In Context)
description: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or from the link text together with its context.
plainLanguageSummary: Link text should clearly describe where it goes. Avoid 'click here' or 'read more' without context.
techniques: G91: Providing link text that describes the purpose of a link
H30: Providing link text that describes the purpose of a link for anchor elements

name: Multiple Ways
description: More than one way is available to locate a Web page within a set of Web pages.
plainLanguageSummary: Provide multiple ways to find pages, such as a site map, search, and navigation menu.
commonFailures: No search functionality on large sites
techniques: G63: Providing a site map
G161: Providing a search function

name: Headings and Labels
description: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.
plainLanguageSummary: Headings and form labels must clearly describe what they're about.
commonFailures: Form labels that don't match their fields
techniques: G130: Providing descriptive headings
G131: Providing descriptive labels

name: Focus Visible
description: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.
plainLanguageSummary: When tabbing through a page, the focused element must have a clearly visible outline or indicator.
commonFailures: Focus indicators that are too subtle
techniques: G149: Using user interface components that are highlighted by the user agent when they receive focus
C15: Using CSS to change the presentation of a user interface component when it receives focus

name: Pointer Gestures
description: All functionality that uses multipoint or path-based gestures can be operated with a single pointer without a path-based gesture.
plainLanguageSummary: If something needs a swipe or pinch gesture, provide a simple alternative like a button.
techniques: Providing controls that do not require complex gestures

name: Pointer Cancellation
description: For functionality that can be operated using a single pointer, at least one of the following is true: no down-event, can abort, can reverse, or essential.
plainLanguageSummary: Actions should happen on mouse-up, not mouse-down, so users can cancel by moving away.
commonFailures: Actions triggered on mousedown or touchstart
techniques: Using click events instead of mousedown events

name: Label in Name
description: For user interface components with labels that include text, the accessible name contains the text that is presented visually.
plainLanguageSummary: The visible label of a button or control must match its accessible name for speech input users.
commonFailures: Button visually says 'Search' but aria-label says 'Find items'
techniques: G208: Including the text of the visible label as part of the accessible name

name: Motion Actuation
description: Functionality that can be operated by device motion or user motion can also be operated by user interface components.
plainLanguageSummary: If shake-to-undo or tilt features exist, provide button alternatives too.
techniques: Providing conventional controls as an alternative to motion actuation

name: Language of Page
description: The default human language of each Web page can be programmatically determined.
plainLanguageSummary: Set the lang attribute on the HTML element so screen readers know what language to use.
techniques: H57: Using the language attribute on the HTML element

name: Language of Parts
description: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined.
plainLanguageSummary: If content includes text in a different language, mark it with a lang attribute.
techniques: H58: Using language attributes to identify changes in the human language

name: On Focus
description: When any user interface component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.
plainLanguageSummary: Just focusing on an element (tabbing to it) should not trigger unexpected actions like page navigation or form submission.
techniques: G107: Using 'activate' rather than 'focus' as a trigger for changes of context

name: On Input
description: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised.
plainLanguageSummary: Changing a form field should not automatically submit the form or navigate to a new page without warning.
commonFailures: Select menus that auto-navigate on change
Radio buttons that submit forms on selection
techniques: G80: Providing a submit button to initiate a change of context

name: Consistent Navigation
description: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple Web pages within a set of Web pages occur in the same relative order each time.
plainLanguageSummary: Navigation menus and other repeated elements should appear in the same order on every page.
commonFailures: Navigation items in different order on different pages
techniques: G61: Presenting repeated components in the same relative order each time they appear

name: Consistent Identification
description: Components that have the same functionality within a set of Web pages are identified consistently.
plainLanguageSummary: The same function should have the same label everywhere. Don't call it 'Search' on one page and 'Find' on another.
techniques: G197: Using labels, names, and text alternatives consistently for content that has the same functionality

name: Error Identification
description: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.
plainLanguageSummary: When a form has errors, clearly identify which fields have errors and explain what went wrong in text.
commonFailures: Errors shown only with color changes
Errors not associated with specific fields
techniques: G83: Providing text descriptions to identify required fields
G84: Providing a text description when the user provides information that is not in the list of allowed values

name: Labels or Instructions
description: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
plainLanguageSummary: Form fields must have visible labels and clear instructions for how to complete them.
techniques: G131: Providing descriptive labels
G184: Providing text instructions at the beginning of a form

name: Error Suggestion
description: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestions are provided to the user.
plainLanguageSummary: When a form error is detected, suggest how to fix it if possible.
commonFailures: Date format errors without showing expected format
techniques: G85: Providing a text description when user input falls outside the required format
G177: Providing suggested correction text

name: Error Prevention (Legal, Financial, Data)
description: For Web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions, submissions are reversible, checked, or confirmed.
plainLanguageSummary: For important actions (purchases, legal agreements), let users review, correct, and confirm before submitting.
commonFailures: No review step before final submission
techniques: G98: Providing the ability for the user to review and correct answers before submitting
G99: Providing the ability to recover deleted information

name: Parsing
description: In content implemented using markup languages, elements have complete start and end tags, are nested according to their specifications, do not contain duplicate attributes, and IDs are unique.
plainLanguageSummary: HTML must be valid — properly nested tags, unique IDs, and no duplicate attributes.
techniques: G134: Validating Web pages
H74: Ensuring that opening and closing tags are used according to specification

name: Name, Role, Value
description: For all user interface components, the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values can be programmatically set.
plainLanguageSummary: Custom widgets must have proper ARIA roles, names, and states so assistive technology understands them.
techniques: ARIA14: Using aria-label to provide an invisible label
ARIA16: Using aria-labelledby to provide a name for user interface controls

name: Status Messages
description: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties so they can be presented to the user without receiving focus.
plainLanguageSummary: Status messages (like 'Item added to cart' or 'Form submitted') must be announced to screen readers without moving focus.
commonFailures: Success/error messages not announced to screen readers
Missing aria-live regions for dynamic content
techniques: ARIA22: Using role=status to present status messages
ARIA19: Using ARIA role=alert or live regions to identify errors
